display: see all the files in the  @.cursor/rules/ folder, I want to integrate them into the CLAUDE.md and other relevant persistent context for my user scope, keep the same directory structure for the `~/.claude` folder as in the @.cursor/rules/ folder if that is suitable.

the @.cursor/rules/general/karpathy_vibes.mdc should definitely be part of the user scope CLAUDE.md, but the contents of @.cursor/rules/python/ should be in the `~/.claude/rules` folder, and apply in the entire user scope but only for python files. Edit those files in order to make them work with claude code properly, if needed.

perfect, now I want to take the skill definitions in the @.cursor/skills/ directory, and based on that I want you to transfer them into the user scope `~/.claude/` folder, either as a skill or a sub-agent. I want you to analyze properly which of the skills in the folder can actually be better used as a sub-agent (e.g.  the code cleaner skill might be a good candidate for a sub-agent), and which of them should remain as a skill. You can ignore the @.cursor/skills/playwright/ skill as we already have this for claude code.

also, I want you to add a python specific rule in the `~/.cursor/rules/` folder, which is about using the `uv` tool from Astral, to manage python virtual environments, handling and managing dependencies etc. Figure out a minimal set of rules to use for all python files. Add this file in that folder.

This is the v0 draft of a book written by my father in @original_draft.md , based on his experiences in climbing the corporate ladder within the management of a large multinational firm in India. I need this entire document to be proofread, correct all errors in spelling and grammar, as well as suggestions for better phrasing and sentence construction wherever applicable. Consider yourself as an expert editor and proofreader at a large publishing house, first break this down into markdown files for each individual chapter, git commit at this state, and then carry out the proofreading and editing for each chapter, and also create at the end a summary markdown document detailing all the edits and changes with respect to the original version, with proper references to locations in the original text. Use as many sub-agents as you need to carry out this big task in as parallel a manner as possible. Also, in the project, create a simple python script that allows us to easily one-shot convert all the markdown files into properly formatter pdf files.
instead of requirements, use `uv` based pyproject.toml instead of requirements.txt , everything else is fine in the plan

try using the `uv` CLI tool to run the @generate_pdf.py script, and generate the final pdf book from all the chapters in the @chapters/ folder. Fix any issues with the python script on the fly, by reading the errors and also checking the final generated pdf.

update the @CLAUDE.md based on the learnings from working on this project, including using `uv` to install dependencies and manage the python virtual environment for running the script. Make it concise.

write a tiny writeup for the @README.md about this project, so that people on the github landing page know what it's about, and how to navigate and access the important parts of the project e.g. the chapters and @EDIT_SUMMARY.md , as well as the current pdf version of the book generated by @generate_pdf.py , add proper hyperlinks etc.
